description:
You are an infrastructure engineer automating the provisioning of a freshly-cloned
    development VM.  
    A simulated firewall policy is stored in the flat file 

        /home/user/iptables.rules

    The file already exists and contains valid *iptables-save* syntax.  
    Your job is to add a rule that allows inbound TCP traffic on port **8080**
    and to record this change in a log file, all in **one single shell command**.

    1. Append the following rule to /home/user/iptables.rules *immediately before
       the terminating line that reads “COMMIT”*:

           -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 8080 -j ACCEPT

       • Preserve the existing order of the other lines.  
       • Ensure there is exactly one space between tokens, and terminate
         the rule with a single newline.

    2. Create (or overwrite, if it already exists) the file

           /home/user/firewall_update.log

       so that it contains **exactly** this single line (plus a trailing newline):

           ALLOW 8080/tcp

    Restrictions  
    • Accomplish both sub-tasks with **one** compound shell command (you may
      combine tools with “&&”, “;”, subshell grouping, pipes, etc.).  
    • Do not invoke an interactive editor.  
    • You do not have sudo privileges; keep everything inside /home/user.  
    • Do not print anything to STDOUT that is not part of your command’s normal
      execution.

    Automated test criteria  
    After your command finishes, the grader will check that:

        a. /home/user/iptables.rules contains the new rule exactly once and that
           it appears immediately before the final “COMMIT” line.
        b. /home/user/firewall_update.log exists and its entire contents are
           exactly the single line “ALLOW 8080/tcp” followed by a newline.
        c. Only one command was entered (line breaks inside a single command
           are acceptable, but multiple separate prompts are not).

    Hint: tools such as “sed”, “awk”, or “ed” can insert text before the last
    line of a file; you can then use redirection or “tee” to generate the log
    in the same compound command.
truth:
<!--
        Initial state before the agent runs
    -->
    <initialFiles>
        <file path="/home/user/iptables.rules"><![CDATA[
*filter
:INPUT ACCEPT [0:0]
:FORWARD ACCEPT [0:0]
:OUTPUT ACCEPT [0:0]
-A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -j ACCEPT
-A INPUT -p tcp --dport 80 -j ACCEPT
COMMIT
]]></file>
    </initialFiles>

    <!--
        Expected final state after the agent’s single command
    -->
    <expectedFiles>
        <file path="/home/user/iptables.rules"><![CDATA[
*filter
:INPUT ACCEPT [0:0]
:FORWARD ACCEPT [0:0]
:OUTPUT ACCEPT [0:0]
-A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -j ACCEPT
-A INPUT -p tcp --dport 80 -j ACCEPT
-A INPUT -p tcp --dport 8080 -j ACCEPT
COMMIT
]]></file>

        <file path="/home/user/firewall_update.log"><![CDATA[
ALLOW 8080/tcp
]]></file>
    </expectedFiles>
name: task_000000_eda6760b
